Interactive QA is certainly an interesting problem and is well-motivated by the paper. However
column314: I don't feel like the bAbI extension is adequately explained. For example
column315: the baseline DMN and MemN2N models on the IQA task are take both statements and question as input and thenestimate an answer. Their task is then fundamentally more difficult from the CAN's because they do not distinguish feedback from the original context; perhaps a more fair approach would be to treat **every** question (both supporting and original questions) as individual instances. Also
column316: how were the supporting questions and the user feedback generated? How many templates / words were used to create them? The dataset creation details are missing
column317: and if space is an issue
column318: a lot of basic exposition on things like GRU / sentence encodings can be cut (or at least greatly shortened) and replaced with pointers to the original papers. Another issue I had is that the model attempts to generate these synthetic questions; if there are just one or two templates
column319: why not just predict the values that fill these templates? So instead of generating Which bedroom
column320: master one or guest one? with an RNN decoder
column321: just predict which or which bedroom... isn't this sufficient? In the end
column322: these just seem like more supporting facts

column1: We propose a novel model for Interactive Question Answering (IQA)
column2: utilizing Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU) networks as encoders for statements and questions
column3: and another GRU as a decoder for outputs. Our approach innovatively employs context-dependent word-level attention to enhance statement representations and question-guided sentence-level attention for improved context modeling. This enables our model to discern when to output an answer or when to generate a supplementary question for further input. When user feedback is available
column4: it is encoded and directly applied to update sentence-level attention to infer the answer. Extensive experiments on QA and IQA datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of our model
column5: showing significant improvements over conventional QA models.

column1: The ultimate goal of Question Answering (QA) research is to develop intelligent systems capable of natural communication with humans
column2: presenting significant challenges in natural language processing and machine learning. Recent successes with sequence-to-sequence models in an encoder-decoder framework (Sutskever et al.
column3: 2014; Cho et al.
column4: 2014) have inspired researchers to apply variants of these models with explicit memory and attention mechanisms to QA tasks
column5: aiming to advance from machine learning to machine reasoning (Sainbayar et al.
column6: 2015; Kumar et al.
column8: 2016). These models typically employ encoders to map statements and questions to fixed-length feature vectors
column9: followed by a decoder to generate outputs. The integration of memory and attention has led to remarkable success on various challenging datasets
column10: including the well-known Facebook bAbI dataset.

column2: existing models exhibit critical limitations. Firstly
column3: they often fail to capture the context-dependent meanings of words. The same word can have different meanings in different contexts
column4: complicating the extraction of essential semantic logic flows in sentences across varying paragraphs. Secondly
column5: many models operate under ideal QA conditions
column6: neglecting scenarios where additional user input is necessary to gather complete information for answering a question. For instance
column8: ambiguity arises when multiple entities are mentioned
column9: making it unclear which entity the user refers to. Such situations frequently occur in human conversations
column10: and effectively managing them is essential for intelligent QA systems.

column2: we introduce the Context-aware Attention Network (CAN)
column3: designed to learn fine-grained representations for input sentences and to facilitate user interaction for comprehensive question understanding. Our model employs two levels of attention—word-level and sentence-level—to compute representations of all input sentences. The context information extracted from the input story influences the attention over each word
column4: guiding the semantic meaning that contributes to sentence representation. Additionally
column5: an interactive mechanism is activated to generate supplementary questions when the model determines that it lacks sufficient information to answer a given question. User feedback is then encoded and utilized to attend to all input sentences for answer inference. Our proposed model CAN can be viewed as an encoder-decoder approach augmented with two-level attention and an interactive mechanism
column6: rendering it self-adaptive.

column1: Our contributions are as follows: (i) We develop a new encoder-decoder model called CAN for question answering with two-level attention. This novel attention mechanism eliminates the need for multiple-hop attention required by previous QA models
column2: enabling the model to recognize when it can output an answer or when it requires additional information. (ii) We enhance the encoder-decoder framework for QA with an interactive mechanism that incorporates user feedback
column3: allowing immediate adjustments to sentence-level attention for answer inference without requiring additional model training. (iii) We introduce a new dataset based on the bAbI dataset
column4: termed ibAbI
column5: specifically designed for IQA tasks. (iv) Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach outperforms state-of-the-art models on both QA and IQA datasets
column6: achieving a 40% improvement over traditional QA models (e.g.
column7: MemN2N and DMN+) on IQA datasets.

column3: this innovative attention mechanism enables our model to discern when it can output an answer readily and when it should generate a supplementary question to activate user interaction. The incorporation of user feedback does not necessitate additional model training

column3: UKnkushman@microsoft.comkatja.hofmann@microsoft.comABSTRACTReinforcement learning is concerned with learning to interact with environmentsthat are initially unknown. State-of-the-art reinforcement learning approaches
column4: such as DQN
column5: are model-free and learn to act effectively across a wide range ofenvironments such as Atari games
column6: but require huge amounts of data. Model-based techniques are more data-efficient
column7: but need to acquire explicit knowledgeabout the environment dynamics or the reward structure.In this paper we take a step towards using model-based techniques in environmentswith high-dimensional visual state space when system dynamics and the rewardstructure are both unknown and need to be learned
column8: by demonstrating that it ispossible to learn both jointly. Empirical evaluation on five Atari games demon-strate accurate cumulative reward prediction of up to 200 frames. We considerthese positive results as opening up important directions for model-based RL incomplex
column9: initially unknown environments.1INTRODUCTIONWhen humans or animals receive reward for taking a particular action in a given situation
column10: the prob-ability is increased that they will act similarly in similar situations in the future. This is describedby principles such as the law of effect (Thorndike
column15: and has inspired a discipline ofartificial intelligence called reinforcement learning (RL
column16: Sutton & Barto (1998)). RL is concernedwith finding optimal behavior policies in order to maximize agents cumulative future reward.Approaches to RL can be divided into model-free and model-based approaches. In model-free ap-proaches
column17: agents learn by trial and error but do not aim to explicitly capture the dynamics of the envi-ronment or the structure of the reward function underlying the environment. State-of-the-art model-free approaches
column22: the value of taking specific actions in a given state
column23: using deep neural networks. The impressiveeffectiveness of these approaches comes from their ability to learn complex policies directly fromhigh-dimensional input (e.g.
column25: model-free approaches re-quire large amounts of training data that have to be collected through direct interactions with theenvironment
column26: which makes them expensive to apply in settings where interactions are costly (suchas most real-world applications). Additionally
column27: model-free RL requires access to reward observa-tions during training
